fact: April 5 is exactly 95 days into the year, which means there are exactly 270 days left until New Year's Eve!
In the Northern Hemisphere, this is prime time for earthworms to go absolutely crazy after spring rain - they wiggle up from underground like slimy party guests!
The sun rises almost exactly due east and sets almost exactly due west around this date, making it super easy to figure out directions without a compass.
Cherry blossom trees in Japan are typically at peak bloom right around April 5, creating pink snow when the petals fall - but only for about one week!
This date falls smack in the middle of Aries season, when the zodiac ram is supposedly making everyone extra energetic and maybe a little stubborn.
In Antarctica, April 5 marks the beginning of the polar night season when the sun starts disappearing for months - talk about the ultimate bedtime!
Migrating hummingbirds are often zooming through North America right now, beating their wings up to 80 times per second as they race north for summer.
The moon phase on April 5 changes every year, but when it's a new moon on this date, you get the darkest skies perfect for seeing shooting stars.
In northern regions, this is when maple trees typically start their sap flow going bonkers - one tree can produce enough sap for a whole gallon of syrup!
April 5 sits exactly two weeks after the spring equinox, which means daylight is winning the battle against darkness by about 3 minutes every single day.
Tadpoles in ponds across temperate zones are usually starting to sprout their back legs right around this time - they're literally growing feet while swimming!
The average temperature difference between morning and afternoon on April 5 can be a whopping 30 degrees Fahrenheit in many places - nature's own roller coaster!
